sound_type: 'keer keer' -- high-pitched, sharp, two-syllable call echoing through old-growth forest at dawn. Flight call during commute between forest and sea. E=4
ecosystem: Nests in old-growth and mature forest on large mossy branches. Feeds at sea. Most extreme habitat split of any PNW bird -- marine feeding, terrestrial nesting.
season: Summer breeder. Nests inland spring-summer. At sea year-round for feeding. Dawn/dusk commute flights.

coordinates.description: Marbled Murrelet in PNW old-growth forest. Olympic Peninsula nesting habitat. Commutes to coastal waters.

taxonomy_note.description: FIFTH ALCIDAE (174-178). Continuing Alcidae streak. Most extreme nesting contrast in the family -- old-growth forest, sometimes 50+ miles inland. Nest not described until 1974. ESA Threatened.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Alcidae: Pigeon Guillemot (174), Rhinoceros Auklet (175), Tufted Puffin (176), Common Murre (177), Marbled Murrelet (178).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Brachyramphus from Greek 'brachy' (short) + 'ramphos' (bill). marmoratus from Latin 'marmoratus' (marbled) -- referring to the marbled breeding plumage. Murrelet: diminutive of 'murre.'
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Brachyramphus -- murrelets. 2 species. Small Alcidae. Marbled and Kittlitz's Murrelets. Old-growth and glacier nesters.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Charadriiformes. Alcidae continuing from degrees 174-177. Fifth consecutive entry.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Fifth Alcidae. First Brachyramphus. Old-growth forest nester. Most extreme habitat contrast in family. Streak continues.

song_metrics.song_duration_s: Each 'keer': 0.15-0.25 seconds. Repeated 2-6 times per sequence.
song_metrics.note_structure: High-pitched, sharp 'keer keer.' Two-syllable. Cuts through forest ambient. Given in flight.
song_metrics.note_types: Flight call (primary -- dawn/dusk commute), alarm call, contact call.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Repeated 'keer keer keer' in flight. 2-6 repetitions per sequence.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp, high-pitched, penetrating. Clear. Surprising in forest context.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Moderate volume. Penetrating in context. Small bird, proportional sound. Context amplifies impact.
song_metrics.tempo: Repetitions at ~2-3 per second during flight.
song_metrics.context: Dawn and dusk commute flights. Old-growth forest canopy. Also at sea.
song_metrics.key_property: 'KEER KEER' in old-growth forest -- seabird in the trees. Nest discovered 1974. Threatened. E=4. v1.49.318.

plumage.overall: Breeding: dark brown above, heavily marbled. Non-breeding: black above, white below, white scapular stripe. Small, chunky seabird.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Breeding: marbled brown overall. Non-breeding: black-and-white with white scapulars. Small. Chunky.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Dramatic. Breeding: brown marbled. Non-breeding: black-and-white. Different enough to confuse beginners.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct, low over water or through forest canopy. Rapid wingbeats. Small wings.

physical_description.overall: Length: 24-25 cm. Weight: 200-270g. Bill: short, dark. Small, chunky seabird.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Small size. Marbled breeding plumage. Fast flight. Forest context in summer.
physical_description.field_id: Small chunky seabird. At sea: look for marbled brown (summer) or black-and-white (winter). In forest: listen for high 'keer keer' at dawn. Often detected by sound only.

nodes.title: 'Keer Keer' -- Seabird in the Forest
nodes.description: High-pitched, sharp flight call echoing through old-growth canopy at dawn and dusk. Penetrating but moderate. Context transforms the experience -- a seabird calling in ancient trees.

nodes.title: Old-Growth Nesting Mystery
nodes.description: Nests on large mossy branches of ancient conifers. 30-60m above ground. No nest structure. One egg. Chick cryptic. Nest not described until 1974 -- last in North America.

nodes.title: Threatened -- Timber Conflict
nodes.description: ESA Threatened (1992). Old-growth dependent. Every acre logged = potential nesting habitat lost. Flagship species in PNW timber wars alongside Northern Spotted Owl.

nodes.title: Forest-Sea Connection
nodes.description: A seabird that needs ancient trees. Commutes 50+ miles between forest and sea. Living proof that marine and terrestrial ecosystems are connected. The forest-sea link.

cross_references.relationship: NESTING CONTRAST! Murre: dense cliff colony. Murrelet: solitary forest branch. Most extreme nesting contrast in any bird family. Both Alcidae.
ALCIDAE STREAK! Puffin: cliff burrow on island. Murrelet: tree branch in forest. Both Alcidae but opposite nesting worlds.
MURRELET PAIR! Marbled (178) and Ancient (179). Two murrelets in sequence. Different genera (Brachyramphus vs Synthliboramphus). Different mysteries.
ALCIDAE OPENS! Guillemot nearshore (174) to Murrelet inland (178). Alcidae nesting habitats span coast to deep forest.
